text: If it is not right, do not do it; if it is not true, do not say it.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: To the best of my judgment, when I look at the human character I see no virtue placed there to counter justice. But I see one to counter pleasure: self-control.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: I have learned to be a friend to myself. Great improvement this indeed. Such a one can never be said to be alone, for know that he who is a friend to himself is a friend to all mankind.
author: Seneca

text: The wise person knows it is fruitless to project hopes and fears on the future. This only leads to forming melodramatic representations in your mind and wasting time.
author: Epictetus

text: A great physicist is always a metaphysicist as well; he has a higher concept of his knowledge and his task.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Do not act as if thou wert going to live ten thousand years. Death hangs over thee. While thou livest, while it is in thy power, be good.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Discipline had vanished from the world. It had been replaced by the catastrophe. We were living in permanent unrest, and no one could trust anyone else.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: If you choose, you are free; if you choose, you need blame no man—accuse no man. All things will be at once according to your mind and according to the Mind of God.
author: Epictetus

text: As men, we bear the seal of superiority, which is very difficult to break, unless we ourselves undermine it [...] (from diary note of 18 April 1939, Kirchhorst, p. 21)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Man, what are you talking about? Me in chains? You may fetter my leg but my will, not even Zeus himself can overpower.
author: Epictetus

text: The more the panic grows, the more uplifting the image of a man who refuses to bow to the terror.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: You have power over your mind - not outside events. Realize this, and you will find strength.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Freedom is based on the anarch's awareness that he can kill himself. He carries this awareness around; it accompanies him like a shadow that he can conjure up. "A leap from this bridge will set me free.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Look well into thyself; there is a source of strength which will always spring up if thou wilt always look.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The Mediterranean is a great homeland, an ancient home. With each new visit I notice it more and more clearly. Does a Mediterranean also exist in the cosmos? (from Near the Saracen tower, Illador, 22 May 1954, p. 152)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: There is never a time when new distraction will not show up; we sow them, so several will grow from the same seed.
author: Seneca

text: It is not that I, as an anarch, reject authority à tout prix. On the contrary, I seek it, and that is precisely why I reserve the right to examine it.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Death is a release from the impressions of the senses, and from desires that make us their puppets, and from the vagaries of the mind, and from the hard service of the flesh.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: This is one of the tragic aspects of modern man, or rather perhaps the true tragic aspect: knowing how to dominate the way, and yet failing in substance. Modern man is perfect even in decline. (from On Grobianism, Europe, p. 147)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: The belly will not listen to advice; it makes demands, it importunes. And yet it is not a troublesome creditor; you can send it away at small cost, provided only that you give it what you owe, not merely all you are able to.
author: Seneca

text: The gaze becomes all the more free the less we look for rank and value in nature. The final answer to every question is: "This is You." (from The Spanish Beetle, p. 223)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Another does wrong. What is that to me? Let him look to it; he has his own disposition, his own activity. I have now what Universal Nature wills me to have, and I do what my own nature wills me to do.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Those who suffer are closer to birth. He pays the greatest tribute, and he pays for the others too. More things change in Stalingrad than in Sedan. (from At the wall of time. Human divisions, p. 95)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: And indeed he who pursues pleasure as good, and avoids pain as evil, is guilty of impiety.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Detailed, meticulous knowledge can be harmful. A lover, a poet, a true sage must be able to see less and more at the same time, they must look with different eyes. (from Mangroves, p. 129)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: What am I doing with my soul? Interrogate yourself, to find out what inhabits your so-called mind and what kind of soul you have now. A child's soul, an adolescent's, a woman's? A tyrant's soul? The soul of a predator—or its prey?
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: This also defines the stature of a thinker: to what extent he has succeeded in surpassing historical man in himself. Being a prophet means knowing much more than the future: the deepest present. (from A Morning in Antibes, p. 284)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Therefore, nothing ought to be unexpected by us. Our minds should be sent forward in advance to meet all problems, and we should consider, not what is wont to happen, but what can happen.
author: Seneca

text: This was the home of the great god Pain, and for the first time I looked through a devilish chink into the depths of his realm. And fresh shells came down all the time.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: One of the causes of the troubles that beset us is the way our lives are guided by the example of others; instead of being set to rights by reason we're seduced by convention.
author: Seneca

text: I am an anarch – not because I despise authority, but because I need it. Likewise, I am not a nonbeliever, but a man who demands something worth believing in.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: He who loves fame considers another man's activity to be his own good; and he who loves pleasure, his own sensations; but he who has understanding, considers his own acts to be his own good.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The more anguish grows in the world, the further away the spaces we go to explore are. We wander like bats in the cave that is the universe. (from Mushrooms, pp. 236-237)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: It makes no difference how important the provocation may be, but into what kind of soul it penetrates. Similarly with fire; it does not matter how great is the flame, but what it falls upon.
author: Seneca

text: He was fleeced, robbed; and yet he was the exploiter. The government, unhesitatingly obliging to the majority, pocketed his taxes, and allowed him to be bled.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: A person teaching and a person learning,' he said, 'should have the same end in view: the improvement of the latter.
author: Seneca

text: The anarch, as I have expounded elsewhere, is the pendant to the monarch; he is as sovereign as the monarch, and also freer since he does not have to rule.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Why not stop trying to prevent posterity being silent about you? You were born to die, and a silent funeral is less bothersome.
author: Seneca

text: There is greater hope in pain than in gifted happiness. (from The blue pilot, p. 316)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: If therefore it be a thing external that causes thy grief, know, that it is not that properly that doth cause it, but thine own conceit and opinion concerning the thing: which thou mayest rid thyself of, when thou wilt.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: In his life, like many before and after him, he had sought comfort and freedom in the forest. Our brother man has often abandoned us, brother tree never has.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: From my great-grandfather: not to have attended schools for the public; to have had good teachers at home, and to realize that this is the sort of thing on which one should spend lavishly.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Toleration of all sides, of which we were so proud, must be seen for what it is – a negative quality. He who has no real belief in anything can certainly be tolerant and to spare; but only intolerance has any force behind it.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Conduct yourself in all matters, grand and public or small and domestic, in accordance with the laws of nature. Harmonizing your will with nature should be your utmost ideal.
author: Epictetus

text: One should be guided less by catastrophes than by one's own internal weather conditions. Even when there was no wind, a hidden cliff could spell disaster; and one could quietly while away time in a cabin in the center of a typhoon.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: If anyone tells you that a certain person speaks ill of you, do not make excuses about what is said of you but answer, "He was ignorant of my other faults, else he would not have mentioned these alone."
author: Epictetus

text: The anarch knows the rules. He has studied them as a historian and goes along with them as a contemporary. Wherever possible, he plays his own game within their framework; this makes the fewest waves.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: The ruler must be a philosopher as well as a king; and he must govern unwillingly, because he loves philosophy better than dominion.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Tell me your relation to pain, and I will tell you who you are!
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Let silence be your general rule; or say only what is necessary and in few words.
author: Epictetus

text: Technology, as a universal, cosmopolitical phenomenon, which inexorably pushes towards globalisation, prepares the world state and, indeed, to a certain extent has already achieved it. The world state is its political counterpart. (p. 67)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Today I escaped all circumstance, or rather I cast out all circumstance, for it was not outside me, but within my judgements.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: Together with a great number of others I had twice paid the piper for inefficient governments. We had carried off neither pay nor glory – just the opposite.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Know, first, who you are, and then adorn yourself accordingly.
author: Epictetus

text: The great solitude of the individual is a hallmark of our times.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Freedom is not procured by a full enjoyment of what is desired, but by controlling the desire.
author: Epictetus

text: In a curious failure of comprehension, I looked alertly about me for possible targets for all this artillery fire, not, apparently, realizing that it was actually ourselves that the enemy gunners were trying for all they were worth to hit.
author: Ernst Jünger

text: My city and my country, as I am Antoninus, is Rome; as I am a man, it is the world.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: The desire to escape does not push man into the void; at every exit something else awaits him. Escape, understood in itself as movement, is fatal. (from Books and cities, Europe, p. 93)
author: Ernst Jünger

text: Remember this, that very little is needed to make a happy life.
author: Marcus Aurelius

text: This indicates how far the law has become dubious. People have a sense of being under foreign occupation, and in this relation the criminal appears a kindred soul.
author: Ernst Jünger
